name: security-kb-v1
description: Clean-room security knowledge base (OWASP Top 10 2021 + key MITRE CWEs) used by the RAG layer to ground finding analysis with cited remediation guidance.
provenanceNote: Clean-room. Every document is authored from PUBLIC standards only: OWASP Top 10 (2021) and MITRE CWE. Summaries/remediation are paraphrased general secure-coding guidance, not copied text. No proprietary content is used.

documents.title: OWASP A01:2021 - Broken Access Control
documents.text: Broken access control lets users act outside their intended permissions: viewing or editing other users' records, accessing admin functions, or escalating privileges. Enforce authorization server-side on every request, deny by default, use centralized checks tied to the authenticated principal, and never rely on hidden fields or client-side controls.

documents.title: OWASP A02:2021 - Cryptographic Failures
documents.text: Cryptographic failures expose sensitive data through weak or missing encryption: cleartext transport, weak hashes for passwords, hardcoded keys, or outdated algorithms. Use TLS everywhere, strong adaptive password hashing (bcrypt/scrypt/argon2), modern ciphers, and managed key storage; classify data and encrypt at rest and in transit.

documents.title: OWASP A03:2021 - Injection
documents.text: Injection occurs when untrusted input is interpreted as part of a command or query (SQL, OS command, LDAP, XPath) or rendered as markup (XSS). Use parameterized queries / prepared statements, safe APIs, allow-list input validation, and context-aware output encoding. Never concatenate user input into an interpreter string.

documents.title: OWASP A04:2021 - Insecure Design
documents.text: Insecure design is a category of missing or ineffective control design, not an implementation bug. Mitigate with threat modeling, secure design patterns, reference architectures, and limiting resource consumption per user. No amount of perfect implementation fixes a flawed design.

documents.title: OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration
documents.text: Security misconfiguration covers insecure defaults, verbose errors, unnecessary features, permissive CORS, and unpatched settings (including XML external entity processing). Harden systematically, disable defaults and debug in production, apply least functionality, and verify configuration with automated checks.

documents.title: OWASP A06:2021 - Vulnerable and Outdated Components
documents.text: Using components with known vulnerabilities exposes the application to public exploits. Maintain an inventory (SBOM), monitor advisories, remove unused dependencies, and patch promptly from trusted sources.

documents.title: OWASP A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures
documents.text: Authentication failures include weak credentials, missing brute-force protection, weak session management, and exposed credentials. Enforce strong/breached-password checks, MFA, rate limiting, secure session IDs, and proper credential storage.

documents.title: OWASP A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
documents.text: Integrity failures arise from trusting code or data without verifying integrity: insecure deserialization, unsigned updates, and compromised CI/CD pipelines. Verify signatures, avoid deserializing untrusted data, and secure the build/deploy supply chain.

documents.title: OWASP A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
documents.text: Insufficient logging and monitoring delays breach detection and response. Log security-relevant events with enough context, protect logs from injection and tampering, centralize them, and alert on suspicious activity.

documents.title: OWASP A10:2021 - Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
documents.text: SSRF lets an attacker make the server send crafted requests to unintended destinations (internal services, cloud metadata endpoints). Validate and allow-list outbound destinations, block internal IP ranges, disable unused URL schemes, and isolate the fetch service.

documents.title: CWE-89: SQL Injection
documents.text: Untrusted input is incorporated into a SQL statement, allowing attackers to read, modify, or destroy data and sometimes execute commands. Remediate with parameterized queries / prepared statements or a vetted ORM; never build SQL by string concatenation. Apply least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: CWE-78: OS Command Injection
documents.text: Untrusted input flows into an OS command, enabling arbitrary command execution. Avoid the shell; pass arguments as a fixed list to exec-style APIs (shell=False), allow-list permitted values, and never interpolate user input into a command string.

documents.title: CWE-94: Code Injection
documents.text: Untrusted input is evaluated as code (eval/exec/dynamic import), giving attackers code execution. Eliminate dynamic evaluation of input; use safe parsers, data-only formats, and explicit dispatch maps instead of eval.

documents.title: CWE-79: Cross-site Scripting (XSS)
documents.text: Untrusted data is included in a web page without proper escaping, letting attackers run script in victims' browsers. Use context-aware output encoding, rely on auto-escaping template engines (avoid raw/|safe on untrusted data), and apply a Content Security Policy.

documents.title: CWE-502: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
documents.text: Deserializing attacker-controlled data (e.g. pickle, native serializers) can instantiate dangerous objects and lead to remote code execution. Do not deserialize untrusted input; prefer data-only formats (JSON) with strict schemas, or sign/verify payloads and restrict permitted types.

documents.title: CWE-611: XML External Entity (XXE)
documents.text: An XML parser that resolves external entities can be abused to read local files, perform SSRF, or cause denial of service. Disable DTD processing and external entity resolution in the parser, or use a hardened parser configuration.

documents.title: CWE-918: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
documents.text: The server fetches a URL derived from user input without restriction, reaching internal systems or cloud metadata. Allow-list destinations, resolve and block private/link-local IP ranges, restrict URL schemes, and avoid following redirects to internal hosts.

documents.title: CWE-22: Path Traversal
documents.text: Untrusted input used to build a file path lets attackers escape the intended directory (../) and read or write arbitrary files. Canonicalize and validate paths against an allow-listed base directory, reject traversal sequences, and use safe path-join APIs.

documents.title: CWE-798: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
documents.text: Credentials embedded in source code are easily extracted and cannot be rotated cleanly. Load secrets from environment variables or a secrets manager, keep them out of version control, and rotate any credential that was ever committed.

documents.title: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
documents.text: Broken or weak algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES) for security purposes can be defeated. Use vetted modern primitives; for passwords use adaptive hashes (bcrypt/scrypt/argon2). Note: MD5/SHA-1 for non-security checksums/cache keys is not a vulnerability.

documents.title: CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation
documents.text: Disabling or mishandling TLS certificate validation enables man-in-the-middle attacks. Always verify certificates and hostnames against a trusted CA store; never disable verification in production.

documents.title: CWE-352: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
documents.text: CSRF tricks an authenticated user's browser into submitting unwanted state-changing requests. Use anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, and require re-authentication for sensitive actions.

documents.title: CWE-601: Open Redirect
documents.text: Redirecting to a user-controlled URL enables phishing and token theft. Allow-list redirect targets or use indirect references; never redirect to a raw user-supplied absolute URL.

documents.title: CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
documents.text: Sensitive data sent over an unencrypted channel (HTTP) can be intercepted. Use TLS for all sensitive traffic, enable HSTS, and disable plaintext protocols.

documents.title: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
documents.text: Verbose errors (stack traces, SQL, secrets) leak internal details that aid attackers. Return generic error messages to clients and log detailed diagnostics server-side only.

documents.title: CWE-863: Incorrect Authorization
documents.text: Authorization logic exists but is implemented incorrectly, allowing access under conditions it should deny (flawed role checks, bypass branches). Centralize authorization, deny by default, and test policy decisions against the authenticated principal and resource owner.

documents.title: CWE-434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
documents.text: Allowing uploads of executable or dangerous file types can lead to code execution or malware hosting. Validate type by content, store outside the web root, randomize names, and serve with safe content-types.

documents.title: CWE-330: Use of Insufficiently Random Values
documents.text: Predictable random values used for tokens, session IDs, or keys can be guessed. Use a cryptographically secure RNG (secrets/os.urandom) for any security-sensitive value, never a general-purpose PRNG.
